elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a simplified model of planar first passage percolation where weights along vertical edges are deterministic.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that the limit shape has a flat edge in the vertical direction if and only if the random distribution of the horizontal edges has an atom at the infimum of its support.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, we present bounds on the upper and lower derivative of the time constant.
elements.sent-2411-004: Classical first passage percolation has a simple definition: Consider the $\Z^2$ lattice and put weights on each edge independently and identically according to some non-negative probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-005: The graph distance now becomes a random metric space and one can study random metric balls at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-006: Cox and Durrett \cite{coxdurrett have shown that under mild assumptions this random ball converges to a deterministic, convex limit shape, which depends on the choice of distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-007: While it is conjectured that the limit shape is differentiable if the distribution is not deterministic and strictly convex if the distribution is continuous, the nature of the limit shape is still almost completely open.
elements.sent-2411-008: There are only a few special cases where one can say something about the limit shape, most notably:.
elements.sent-2411-009: If the distribution is deterministic, then the limit shape is a dilation of the $\ell^1$-diamond.
elements.sent-2411-010: If the distribution is supported on $[1,\infty)$ and has an atom at $1$ of mass at least $1/2$, then the limit shape has a flat edge near the main diagonal and is differentiable at the endpoints of that edge (see \cite{durrettliggett and \cite{marchand ).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: For each $k$, there exists $\eps>0$ such that a random variable supported on $[1,1+\eps]$ has a limit shape that is not a polygon with $k$ or less edges (see \cite{coalescence , Theorem~1.4).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Properties of the limit shape are of particular interest because they yield information about the geodesics (see \cite[Lemma~4.6]{coalescence ) and the existence of bi-infinite geodesics \cite{bigeodesics which has implications for the disordered Ising ferromagnet.}}
